# Riverside Library evening-study pilot

Riverside Library is considering a six-week evening-study pilot for adults who
want a quiet, dependable place to work. The library is open on weekdays from
09:00 to 20:00. The quiet room has twenty-four seats, reliable power outlets,
and a door that reduces noise from the children’s area. It does not have a
dedicated tutor, food service, or reserved parking. Evening bus delays are a
frequent reason that visitors arrive after the advertised start time.

The proposed pilot runs Tuesdays and Thursdays from 18:00 to 19:30. Two staff
members are normally on duty after 17:00. One staff member can offer a short
orientation while the other remains at the public desk. Volunteers may greet
visitors and restock materials, but they may not collect sensitive information,
make eligibility decisions, or promise services that the library has not
approved. Every public notice must state what is offered, where it happens, how
long it lasts, and that participation is free.

The pilot’s first goal is to make it easier for adult learners to begin work.
The second goal is to understand whether the library can offer the program
without disrupting normal desk service. The pilot is not designed to prove that
attendance causes learning gains. Weather, examination periods, bus service,
and local events all affect who arrives. The evaluation will therefore report
descriptive trends and staff observations rather than causal conclusions.

At the start of each session, staff will place a one-page orientation at the
desk. The page explains where to sit, where to find the first exercise, how to
ask for help, and how to borrow headphones. It is written in plain language and
also available as an accessible digital document. The digital document uses
headings, descriptive links, readable contrast, and no instructions that rely
only on colour. A visitor who arrives late may take the page and join quietly;
no explanation or penalty is required.

The room policy is simple. Visitors use headphones for audio, keep calls outside
the room, and ask staff before moving furniture. If all twenty-four seats are
occupied, staff offer a place in the public reading area and mark the session as
having overflow. Overflow is an operational signal, not a reason to turn away a
visitor. Staff should also note interruptions that make the quiet room less
usable, such as maintenance work or repeated door noise.

The library will track attendance using a tally rather than names. It will
separately tally first-time visitors, returning visitors when they volunteer
that information, and overflow. A small anonymous feedback card asks three
questions: Was the room quiet enough? Were the materials usable? What time
would work better? The card includes an optional open response. It must not ask
for names, contact details, immigration status, disability status, or other
sensitive information.

Staff will run a five-minute debrief after each session. The debrief records
whether the orientation was available, whether desk service was interrupted,
whether accessibility problems were reported, whether overflow occurred, and
one improvement to test next time. The manager reviews these notes weekly. A
change is recorded with its date so that later readers do not confuse a policy
change with a change in attendance.

The communication plan has three channels: a printed notice near the entrance,
the library website, and a short message for partner organisations. Each uses
the same factual wording. The notice should not imply tutoring, certification,
child care, food, transportation, or guaranteed seating. The website includes a
contact route for accessibility requests, but it does not promise an
accommodation before staff confirm it can be provided.

The pilot begins with one baseline week in which the room remains open but the
orientation card is not offered. Weeks two through five use the card. Week six
is reserved for review. The manager compares the weekly tallies, reads the
anonymous comments, and asks staff whether the program was manageable. A
summary distinguishes observed facts from recommendations. For example,
“overflow occurred on three Tuesdays” is an observed fact; “add another study
night” is a recommendation that requires an owner decision.

Possible follow-up decisions include changing the start time, adding a second
orientation page, reserving a nearby overflow table, or ending the pilot. The
decision should weigh attendance, repeat attendance, overflow, accessibility
feedback, and staff workload together. A single busy night should not decide
the program’s future. The library’s stated aim is a calm, practical service
that helps visitors begin work without creating promises it cannot keep.

## Operations appendix: opening each session

Before each pilot session, the staff member assigned to orientation checks that
the room is unlocked, the orientation cards are present, the accessible digital
copy can be opened, and the feedback cards are at the exit. The desk staff
member checks the sign near the entrance for accuracy and removes any outdated
notice. These checks are short, but they prevent a common reporting error: a
visitor cannot use a service that was described but not actually available. If
an item is missing, staff record it in the debrief and use the least disruptive
fallback that remains within the approved policy.

The opening check does not require a list of participants. Staff should not ask
visitors why they are studying, what course they take, or whether they have a
diagnosis. A visitor can choose a seat, read the orientation, and begin. If a
visitor asks for help outside staff expertise, the appropriate response is to
identify a known library resource or say that staff do not have enough
information. The program is a place to study, not an assessment or advising
service.

## Operations appendix: accessibility and communication

Accessibility requests are handled with care. A staff member may ask what
would make the visit workable, but should not request more personal detail than
is necessary. The response can include moving a chair, describing where a
resource is located, or offering the digital orientation. It cannot include a
promise that a specialist, device, or building modification will be available
unless that has been confirmed. The debrief notes the barrier and the general
category of response without identifying the visitor.

All three communication channels are reviewed together. A discrepancy between
the printed notice and the website is treated as a problem because visitors may
rely on either one. Staff use the approved factual wording: evening study is a
quiet place to work, it runs at the stated time, seats may be limited, and it
does not include tutoring. Partner organisations may share the notice, but they
should not rewrite it to add benefits that the library has not approved.

## Operations appendix: interpreting the pilot

Attendance is useful but incomplete. A session with fewer people may still be
valuable if participants can work without interruption, and a full room may be
unsuccessful if desk service collapses. The weekly review therefore reads the
tallies alongside accessibility comments, overflow, and staff notes. The
manager should explicitly mark a conclusion as tentative when the evidence is
thin. For example, two busy Tuesdays do not establish that Tuesday is the best
night for every visitor.

When a change is tested, the manager writes the change before the session. A
useful entry says what changed, why it changed, and what observation would make
the team revisit it. This small discipline keeps the pilot understandable when
several changes occur near each other. It also makes the final summary more
honest: readers can see whether an apparent improvement followed a new policy,
a holiday week, or a one-time event.

## Operations appendix: close of session

At 19:20, staff give a quiet reminder that the room closes at 19:30. The
reminder should not shame visitors or demand that they disclose why they need
more time. At closing, staff count uncollected feedback cards, secure any paper
that contains comments, and return shared materials to the desk. The room is
then checked for obvious hazards and furniture is returned to its usual layout.
No participant list is created during this process.

The manager reviews feedback in aggregate. A comment about a broken outlet is
an operational issue to route to maintenance. A comment about noise is a signal
to compare against staff observations and scheduled building activity. A
comment asking for tutoring is evidence of demand, not evidence that tutoring
was offered. The final report should preserve that distinction and should list
any unanswered question that requires a future owner decision.

## Operations appendix: decision scenarios

If attendance is steady, overflow is rare, and staff workload is manageable,
the manager may recommend continuing the schedule. If overflow is frequent,
the manager may evaluate a second room or a different time, subject to staffing
and accessibility review. If the room is consistently underused, the manager
may test a different communication channel before ending the program. None of
these responses is automatic. Each is a proposal whose expected benefit,
operational cost, and uncertainty should be stated plainly.

If a serious accessibility or safety problem occurs, staff pause the affected
part of the pilot and notify the manager. They do not improvise a policy that
could create a new barrier. The report records the date, the general nature of
the problem, the immediate safe action, and the follow-up owner. This approach
is intentionally conservative: the program values reliable access and honest
communication over a headline attendance number.
